blurb: Classic large-flowered climber — sheets of velvety violet-purple on a sunny trellis.
sun: Full sun–part shade — top in sun, roots shaded
water: Moderate, consistent; keep roots cool & mulched
spread: Twines by leaf-stalks; clumping crown — not invasive/running
seasons: Showy blooms (spring or summer by pruning group); some give fluffy silver seedheads into fall/winter
toxic: Irritant/toxic if eaten; sap can irritate skin — caution with kids/pets. AVOID Clematis orientalis (yellow orange-peel clematis) — a CO List B noxious weed (all subspecies & cultivars)

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zone 4 (RHS H6, to about -20°C) — well within the Front Range's zone 5b–6a. 'Jackmanii' and its large-flowered Group 3 relatives are among the toughest clematis for Colorado; they die back to the ground in hard winters but reliably regrow from the crown each spring. This is not a problem — it's the expected and desirable behavior for a Group 3 vine.
care.sun: Best in full sun to part shade, with 6+ hours of direct light for good flowering. In practice, the classic rule applies on the Front Range: sun on the top (the flowering stems), shade on the roots. Afternoon shade from a wall, low shrubs, or a layer of pebbles and mulch at the base keeps the root zone cool and moist, which clematis demand.
care.soil: Moist but well-drained, moderately fertile soil — adapts to clay, loam, chalk, or sand provided it doesn't waterlog. On Colorado's alkaline clay-loam, work in generous compost at planting to improve drainage and moisture retention. Clematis are not fussy about pH (tolerate acid to alkaline) but hate standing water, especially in winter.
care.water: Moderate and consistent — keep the root zone evenly moist, never waterlogged. Deep, infrequent watering (every 5–7 days in summer heat) is better than frequent shallow watering. Mulch generously (3–4 in of wood chips or gravel) to retain moisture and buffer soil temperature; pull mulch away from the crown to prevent rot. Newly planted vines need more frequent water until established.
care.spacing: Space plants 18–24 in from a wall or fence, allowing airflow and room to root outward. Mature Group 3 vines reach 8–12 ft (2.5–4 m) tall with a 2–3 ft spread. Allow at least 3 ft between plants on a shared support; one plant can cover a 6–8 ft trellis panel. Provide a trellis, wire framework, or open shrub to twine through — clematis grip by leaf-stalks, not suckers.
care.depth: Plant the crown 2–3 in (5–8 cm) deeper than it sat in its nursery pot. This deep planting is critical for clematis: if the vine develops clematis wilt (a fungal wilt that blackens and kills stems suddenly), buried buds below the soil surface survive and regenerate new growth. Without deep planting, wilt can kill the whole plant. Water in well at planting.
care.bloom: Late June through September on the Front Range, with the main flush in July–August on current season's growth. Spent flowers may develop wispy silver-grey seedheads that persist into fall and add texture. Some cultivars in this group can give a lighter second flush after a late-spring cutback.
care.feeding: Feed in spring as growth resumes with a balanced, slow-release fertilizer or a top-dressing of compost. A second application in early summer supports the long bloom season. Avoid heavy nitrogen, which promotes leafy growth over flowers. Mulch annually in spring.
care.maintenance: Group 3 hard prune every year in late winter or early spring (February–March on the Front Range), as soon as the lowest buds show signs of swelling. Cut all stems back to 6–12 in (15–30 cm) above soil level, just above a healthy pair of buds. This sounds severe but is correct — the vine flowers only on new growth made that season. First year after planting: cut back hard to 12 in to force multiple basal shoots. As the vine grows, tie stems evenly across the support; group 3 vines can also be left to scramble through a large shrub and pruned back hard in spring.
care.troubles: Clematis wilt is the most alarming issue — stems suddenly blacken and collapse, sometimes overnight. Caused by a fungal pathogen; the solution is to cut the affected stems to the ground immediately and dispose of them (don't compost). Deep planting (see 'depth') provides underground buds that regrow. Aphids and earwigs may cluster on young shoots; check regularly. Scale, snails, and caterpillars are occasional visitors. In Colorado's dry air, fungal leaf diseases are rare. Avoid Clematis orientalis and all orange-peel/yellow clematis — they are CO List B noxious weeds.
care.companions: Plant low, shade-giving companions at the base to keep roots cool: hardy geraniums, catmint, salvia, or lavender. Clematis looks spectacular paired with climbing roses (the violet blooms of 'Jackmanii' are a classic match for pink or cream roses) or scrambling through a large deciduous shrub like lilac or Wood's rose.
